title: Print preorder traversal (recursive)
description: Print the preorder traversal (Root -> Left -> Right) of a binary tree using recursion

Final output: 1 2 4 5 3 6

approaches.name: Approach 1: Basic Recursive Traversal
approaches.summary: Directly implement the preorder definition: process the root node first, then recursively traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree. This is the most natural recursive solution that mirrors the problem's recursive structure.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (worst-case O(n) for skewed trees)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Preorder Traversal of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of preorder traversal using recursion
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to print the preorder traversal of a binary tree using recursion. The intuition is that preorder traversal follows the natural recursive structure of a tree: process the root node first, then recursively traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree. This mirrors the definition of preorder traversal (Root → Left → Right). The algorithm starts at the root, prints its value, then recursively calls itself on the left child, then the right child. The base case occurs when the current node is null (no subtree to traverse). Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as each node is visited exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height, representing the maximum depth of the recursion stack. In worst-case skewed trees, this becomes O(n). This solution directly implements the problem statement and serves as the foundation for understanding tree traversal patterns. Common mistakes include forgetting the base case (leading to infinite recursion), incorrect order of recursive calls (must be left before right for preorder), and not handling null nodes properly. The code pattern is clean and demonstrates fundamental recursion principles applied to tree structures.

    Step 3.3.3: Right child of 6 is null, return
Final output: 1 2 4 5 3 6

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Traversal Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that visits nodes in preorder sequence
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the preorder traversal logic. The intuition is that a tree can be naturally traversed by processing the current node, then recursively processing its left and right subtrees. This function takes a tree node as input and performs the three-step preorder operation: process current node, traverse left subtree, traverse right subtree. The base case is when the node is null (no tree to traverse). The algorithm follows: 1. If node is null, return (base case). 2. Process current node (print/store value). 3. Recursively call function on left child. 4. Recursively call function on right child. Time complexity for this function is O(n) where n is nodes in the subtree rooted at the input node. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. This sub-routine is the essential component of the full solution - the main function simply calls this with the root node. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling, swapping left/right traversal order, and not returning after base case. The function demonstrates how recursion elegantly handles tree structures by breaking problems into smaller identical subproblems.

examples.input: Node with value 10, left child 5, right child 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Function called with node 10 (not null)
Step 2: Process node 10, print 10
Step 3: Recursive call with left child (node 5)
  Step 3.1: Node 5 not null
  Step 3.2: Process node 5, print 5
  Step 3.3: Recursive call with left child of 5 (null)
    Step 3.3.1: Node null, return immediately
  Step 3.4: Recursive call with right child of 5 (null)
    Step 3.4.1: Node null, return immediately
  Step 3.5: Return to node 10 call
Step 4: Recursive call with right child (node 15)
  Step 4.1: Node 15 not null
  Step 4.2: Process node 15, print 15
  Step 4.3: Recursive call with left child of 15 (null)
    Step 4.3.1: Node null, return
  Step 4.4: Recursive call with right child of 15 (null)
    Step 4.4.1: Node null, return
  Step 4.5: Return to node 10 call
Step 5: Function completes, total output: 10 5 15

ladders.title: Core Logic — Preorder Sequence Definition
ladders.desc: Understanding and implementing the Root→Left→Right traversal order
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental logic that defines preorder traversal: process the root node first, then the left subtree, then the right subtree. The intuition is that this order allows us to visit nodes in a depth-first manner while always processing parent nodes before their children. This is particularly useful for operations that need parent information before child information (like expression tree evaluation or copying trees). The algorithm is straightforward: 1. Visit/process the current node. 2. Recursively apply the same logic to the left subtree. 3. Recursively apply the same logic to the right subtree. The key insight is that this definition is recursive by nature - the traversal of a tree is defined in terms of traversals of its subtrees. Time complexity analysis shows this processes each node exactly once. Space complexity depends on implementation but is typically O(h) for recursion. This core logic is what makes the recursive solution so elegant - it directly mirrors the problem definition. Common mistakes include confusing preorder with inorder or postorder, not understanding the recursive nature, and attempting iterative solutions prematurely. Understanding this sequence is crucial for the recursive implementation.

examples.input: Tree: Root=A, Left=B, Right=C
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process root node A
Step 2: Apply preorder to left subtree (node B)
  Step 2.1: Process node B (root of this subtree)
  Step 2.2: Left subtree of B is empty
  Step 2.3: Right subtree of B is empty
Step 3: Apply preorder to right subtree (node C)
  Step 3.1: Process node C
  Step 3.2: Left subtree of C is empty
  Step 3.3: Right subtree of C is empty
Sequence: A (root) then B (left) then C (right)

examples.input: Tree: Root=1, Left subtree has root=2 with children 4 and 5, Right=3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process root 1
Step 2: Apply preorder to left subtree (rooted at 2)
  Step 2.1: Process node 2
  Step 2.2: Apply preorder to left subtree of 2 (node 4)
    Step 2.2.1: Process node 4
    Step 2.2.2: No left subtree

    Step 2.3.2: No left subtree
    Step 2.3.3: No right subtree
Step 3: Apply preorder to right subtree (node 3)
  Step 3.1: Process node 3
  Step 3.2: No left subtree
  Step 3.3: No right subtree
Sequence: 1 (root) then entire left subtree (2,4,5) then right subtree (3)

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Node Structure and Recursion
ladders.desc: Understanding binary tree nodes and how recursion applies to tree structures
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental building blocks needed for tree traversal: the tree node structure and the concept of recursion applied to trees. A binary tree node typically contains: 1. A value/data element, 2. A reference/pointer to the left child node, 3. A reference/pointer to the right child node. The intuition is that trees are recursive data structures - each node is the root of its own subtree. This self-similarity makes recursion a natural fit for tree operations. When we apply recursion to trees, we: 1. Handle the base case (null node, representing an empty tree), 2. Process the current node, 3. Make recursive calls on the subtrees (left and right). The algorithm for any tree operation follows this pattern. Time complexity for operations on trees using recursion is typically O(n) as we visit each node. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack. This building block is essential because without understanding nodes and tree recursion, the traversal algorithm makes no sense. Common mistakes include not understanding null represents empty tree, confusing node references with node values, and not seeing the recursive structure. Real tree implementations use classes/structs with value, left, and right attributes.

examples.input: Node class with value=10, left=Node(5), right=Node(15)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with value 10
Step 2: Create left child node with value 5
  - This node has left=null, right=null
Step 3: Create right child node with value 15
  - This node has left=null, right=null
Step 4: Connect them: root.left points to node 5, root.right points to node 15
Step 5: Visualize structure:
    10
   /  \
  5    15
Step 6: Recursive view: Root 10 with left subtree rooted at 5 and right subtree rooted at 15

examples.input: Node with value 1, left child with value 2, left child of 2 has value 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node 1 (root)
Step 2: Create node 2
Step 3: Create node 3
Step 4: Connect: node1.left = node2, node2.left = node3
Step 5: Structure:
    1
   /
  2
 /
3
Step 6: Recursive perspective:
- Tree rooted at 1 has left subtree rooted at 2
- Tree rooted at 2 has left subtree rooted at 3
- Tree rooted at 3 has empty left and right subtrees
Step 7: Each node follows same pattern: value + left reference + right reference

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Function Calls and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Understanding recursive function calls and termination conditions
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations needed for recursion: function calls and base cases. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by having functions call themselves with smaller inputs until they reach a trivial case (base case). For tree traversal, the base case is when we reach a null node (empty tree). The algorithm pattern is: 1. Check if base case reached (node is null). 2. If base case, return (do nothing). 3. Otherwise, process current node. 4. Make recursive calls on smaller subproblems (left and right subtrees). Each recursive call works on a smaller tree (subtree), guaranteeing progress toward base cases. Time complexity depends on number of function calls (O(n) for trees). Space complexity is the maximum depth of recursive calls (O(h)). This operation is fundamental because without proper base cases, recursion would continue infinitely. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases, having incorrect base conditions, and not understanding that each call gets its own execution context. The key insight is that recursion breaks problems into identical but smaller subproblems until they become trivial to solve.

examples.input: Function traverse(node): if node null return; print node.value; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Call traverse(root) where root has value 10
Step 2: Node not null, so print 10
Step 3: Call traverse(node.left) - this is a NEW function call
  Step 3.1: In new call, node has value 5
  Step 3.2: Print 5
  Step 3.3: Call traverse(node.left) where node.left is null
    Step 3.3.1: New call with null node
    Step 3.3.2: Base case reached, return immediately
  Step 3.4: Call traverse(node.right) where node.right is null
    Step 3.4.1: Base case, return
  Step 3.5: Return to original call
Step 4: Call traverse(node.right) - another new call
  Step 4.1: Node has value 15
  Step 4.2: Print 15
  Step 4.3: Call traverse(left) - null, return
  Step 4.4: Call traverse(right) - null, return
Step 5: Original call completes

examples.input: Same function with single node tree (value 7)
examples.output: Single recursive call with base cases
examples.trace:
Step 1: Call traverse(root) with node value 7
Step 2: Node not null, print 7
Step 3: Call traverse(node.left) where node.left is null
  Step 3.1: New call with null
  Step 3.2: Base case, return immediately
Step 4: Call traverse(node.right) where node.right is null
  Step 4.1: New call with null
  Step 4.2: Base case, return
Step 5: Original call completes
Total: 3 function calls (1 with node, 2 with null)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Binary Tree?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic tree data structure
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: understanding what a binary tree is. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes, where each node has at most two children: left and right. The intuition is that it's like a family tree or organizational chart, but with each person having at most two direct reports. The top node is called the root. Nodes with no children are leaves. The key properties: 1. Each node has 0, 1, or 2 children. 2. There is exactly one root node. 3. Each node (except root) has exactly one parent. 4. The tree is connected (no disconnected parts). Binary trees are used because they naturally represent hierarchical relationships and allow efficient search/access when organized properly. They're fundamental in computer science for representing expressions, organizing data, and solving problems recursively. This foundation is essential because without understanding what a tree is, traversal makes no sense. Common mistakes include confusing trees with linear structures, not understanding parent-child relationships, and thinking trees must be balanced. Real-world analogies include family trees (but limited to two children per parent) or decision trees (yes/no branches).

examples.input: Simple binary tree with root A, left child B, right child C
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with root node A
Step 2: A has two children: left child B and right child C
Step 3: B and C are child nodes of A
Step 4: B and C have no children (they are leaves)
Step 5: Visual representation:
    A
   / \
  B   C
Step 6: Properties:
- A is root
- B is left child of A
- C is right child of A
- B and C are leaves
- Each node has max 2 children
- Tree has 3 nodes

examples.input: Tree with root 10, left child 5, left child of 5 is 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Root node is 10
Step 2: 10 has left child 5 (right child is null)
Step 3: Node 5 has left child 2 (right child is null)
Step 4: Node 2 has no children
Step 5: Structure:
    10
   /
  5
 /
2
Step 6: Relationships:
- 10 is root
- 5 is child of 10 and parent of 2
- 2 is child of 5 and leaf node
- This is a 3-level tree
- Each node still has max 2 children (some are null)

testCases.expected: Yes, if each has max 2 children

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Stack-Based
approaches.summary: Use an explicit stack to simulate the recursion call stack. Push nodes in reverse order (right then left) to maintain preorder sequence. This avoids recursion overhead and potential stack overflow for deep trees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative Preorder Traversal Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of iterative preorder traversal using an explicit stack.
ladders.explanation:
The iterative stack-based approach to preorder traversal simulates the recursive call stack explicitly. Instead of relying on the system's call stack (which can overflow for deep trees), we maintain our own stack data structure. The key insight is that preorder traversal processes nodes in Root → Left → Right order. To achieve this iteratively, we start by pushing the root node onto the stack. Then, while the stack is not empty, we pop a node, process it (print its value), and push its children onto the stack in reverse order (right child first, then left child). This reverse order ensures that when we pop nodes from the stack, the left child gets processed before the right child, maintaining the correct preorder sequence.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If the root is null, return.
2. Create an empty stack and push the root node onto it.
3. While the stack is not empty:
   a. Pop a node from the stack and process it (e.g., print its value).
   b. If the node has a right child, push it onto the stack.
   c. If the node has a left child, push it onto the stack.

Time complexity: O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as each node is pushed and popped exactly once.
Space complexity: O(n) in the worst case (e.g., a skewed tree) where the stack holds all nodes.

Connection to L1: This full solution builds upon the core stack management subroutine from L1, integrating it with tree node processing.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to check for null root at the start.
- Pushing children in incorrect order (left then right) which would yield a different traversal order.
- Not handling empty trees properly.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def iterative_preorder(root):
    if root is None:
        return
    stack = [root]
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        print(node.val)  # Process node
        if node.right:
            stack.append(node.right)
        if node.left:
            stack.append(node.left)

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack = [1].
Step 2: Pop node=1, print 1. Push right child 3, then left child 2. Stack = [3, 2].
Step 3: Pop node=2, print 2. No children. Stack = [3].
Step 4: Pop node=3, print 3. No children. Stack = [].
Output: 1 2 3.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 (left: 4, right: 5), right: 3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [1].
Step 2: Pop 1, print 1. Push 3, then 2. Stack = [3, 2].
Step 3: Pop 2, print 2. Push 5, then 4. Stack = [3, 5, 4].
Step 4: Pop 4, print 4. No children. Stack = [3, 5].
Step 5: Pop 5, print 5. No children. Stack = [3].
Step 6: Pop 3, print 3. No children. Stack = [].
Output: 1 2 4 5 3.

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 (left: 4, right: null), right: 3 (left: null, right: 5))

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Management for Node Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that manages stack operations for tree traversal.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the iterative loop that drives the traversal. The stack serves as our manual call stack, storing nodes to be processed. The algorithm repeatedly pops a node, processes it, and pushes its children in reverse order. The intuition is that by pushing the right child before the left, we ensure the left child is processed next (since stacks are LIFO—last in, first out). This mimics the recursive preorder behavior where after processing the root, we traverse the left subtree fully before the right.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty stack.
2. Push the root node onto the stack.
3. While stack is not empty:
   a. Pop the top node.
   b. Process the node (e.g., store its value).
   c. If the node has a right child, push it onto the stack.
   d. If the node has a left child, push it onto the stack.

Time complexity: O(n) as each node is processed once.
Space complexity: O(n) for the stack.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is the engine of the full solution; L0 adds tree structure handling around it.

Common mistakes:
- Using a queue instead of a stack, which would change traversal order.
- Not pushing children in reverse order, leading to incorrect output.
- Infinite loops if stack management is flawed.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def stack_traversal(root):
    stack = []
    if root:
        stack.append(root)
    result = []
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        result.append(node.val)
        if node.right:
            stack.append(node.right)
        if node.left:
            stack.append(node.left)
    return result

examples.input: Root: A (left: B, right: C), process = collect values
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [A].
Step 2: Pop A, result = [A]. Push C, then B. Stack = [C, B].
Step 3: Pop B, result = [A, B]. No children. Stack = [C].
Step 4: Pop C, result = [A, B, C]. No children. Stack = [].
Output: [A, B, C].

examples.input: Root: 1 (left: 2 (left: 4), right: 3), process = collect values
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [1].
Step 2: Pop 1, result = [1]. Push 3, then 2. Stack = [3, 2].
Step 3: Pop 2, result = [1, 2]. Push 4. Stack = [3, 4].
Step 4: Pop 4, result = [1, 2, 4]. No children. Stack = [3].
Step 5: Pop 3, result = [1, 2, 4, 3]. No children. Stack = [].
Output: [1, 2, 4, 3].

testCases.input: Root: A (left: B (right: C), right: D (left: E))

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reverse Order Pushing for Preorder
ladders.desc: Understand why pushing right child before left child achieves preorder traversal.
ladders.explanation:
The central insight of the iterative stack approach is the reverse order pushing strategy. In preorder traversal, we visit the root, then the left subtree, then the right subtree. When using a stack (which is LIFO—last in, first out), items pushed last are popped first. Therefore, to ensure the left child is processed before the right child, we push the right child onto the stack first, then the left child. This way, when we pop nodes, the left child (pushed last) comes out first, aligning with the preorder sequence.

Intuition: Imagine the recursive call: preorder(root) would call preorder(left) before preorder(right). In our stack simulation, by pushing right then left, we delay the right subtree's processing until after the left subtree is fully explored, mimicking the recursion.

Step-by-step logic:
1. After processing a node, consider its children.
2. Push the right child onto the stack (if exists).
3. Push the left child onto the stack (if exists).
4. This order ensures left child is on top of the stack, so it gets processed next.

Time complexity: O(1) per node for push operations.
Space complexity: O(1) extra per child push.

Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the stack management loop; L1 implements it within the full traversal.

Common mistakes:
- Pushing left then right, resulting in a different traversal order (e.g., root, right, left).
- Not handling null children, which could cause errors.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def push_children(node, stack):
    if node.right:
        stack.append(node.right)
    if node.left:
        stack.append(node.left)

examples.input: Node: P (left: Q, right: R), stack initially empty
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process node P.
Step 2: Push right child R. Stack = [R].
Step 3: Push left child Q. Stack = [R, Q].
Now Q is on top, so it will be processed next, maintaining preorder.

examples.input: Node: 1 (left: 2, right: null), stack = []
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process node 1.
Step 2: Right child is null, skip.
Step 3: Push left child 2. Stack = [2].
Only left child pushed, so it's processed next.

testCases.input: Node: A (left: B, right: C), stack = []
Node: X (left: null, right: Y), stack = []
Node: Z (left: W, right: null), stack = [A]
Node: M (left: null, right: null), stack = []
Node: alpha (left: beta, right: gamma), stack = [delta]

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Data Structure Operations
ladders.desc: Learn stack operations (push, pop, peek) and their LIFO behavior.
ladders.explanation:
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. The last element added is the first one removed. Core operations include push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing). In Python, a list can simulate a stack using append() for push and pop() for pop. The intuition for using a stack in tree traversal is that it naturally reverses order: by pushing items in one order, they are popped in the reverse order, which helps control the sequence of node processing.

Key operations:
- Push: Add an element to the top of the stack.
- Pop: Remove and return the top element.
- Peek: Return the top element without removing it.
- IsEmpty: Check if the stack is empty.

Time complexity: O(1) for push, pop, and peek.
Space complexity: O(n) for storing elements.

Connection to L2: Stacks enable the reverse order pushing strategy; L2 uses this to achieve preorder traversal.

Common mistakes:
- Using pop(0) instead of pop() on a list, which makes it a queue (FIFO).
- Not checking if stack is empty before popping, leading to errors.
- Confusing stack with other data structures like queues or arrays.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
stack = []           # Initialize empty stack
stack.append(5)      # Push 5
stack.append(10)     # Push 10
top = stack.pop()    # Pop returns 10
if stack:            # Check if not empty
    peek_val = stack[-1]  # Peek at top element

examples.input: Operations: push A, push B, pop, push C, pop, pop
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push A → stack = [A].
Step 2: Push B → stack = [A, B].
Step 3: Pop → returns B, stack = [A].
Step 4: Push C → stack = [A, C].
Step 5: Pop → returns C, stack = [A].
Step 6: Pop → returns A, stack = [].
Output: B, C, A.

examples.input: Operations: push 1, push 2, peek, pop, peek
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 1 → stack = [1].
Step 2: Push 2 → stack = [1, 2].
Step 3: Peek → returns 2, stack unchanged = [1, 2].
Step 4: Pop → returns 2, stack = [1].
Step 5: Peek → returns 1, stack = [1].
Output: 2, 1.

testCases.input: Operations: push A, push B, push C, pop, peek
Operations: push hello, push world, pop, push there, pop
Operations: push alpha, push beta, pop, peek, pop
testCases.expected: First pop: beta, peek: alpha, second pop: alpha

testCases.input: Operations: push 0, push -1, push 1, pop, pop, pop
Operations: push cat, push dog, pop, push fish, pop, pop
Operations: push 99, push 88, peek, pop, peek

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Access and Child Navigation
ladders.desc: Access a tree node's value and check/retrieve its left and right children.
ladders.explanation:
In a binary tree, each node contains a value and references to its left and right children. The basic operations involve accessing the node's data and navigating to its children. This is fundamental for any tree traversal algorithm. Typically, a node is represented as an object with attributes like 'val', 'left', and 'right', where 'left' and 'right' point to other nodes or are null if no child exists.

Key operations:
- Access value: node.val
- Check left child: if node.left is not null
- Check right child: if node.right is not null
- Retrieve left child: node.left
- Retrieve right child: node.right

Intuition: Traversal algorithms rely on these operations to move through the tree structure. By checking children, we determine the next nodes to process, and by accessing values, we perform the desired action (e.g., printing).

Time complexity: O(1) per access.
Space complexity: O(1) extra.

Connection to L3: These operations are used when pushing children onto the stack; L3's stack manages the nodes accessed here.

Common mistakes:
- Not handling null children, leading to null pointer errors.
- Confusing node values with node references.
- Assuming every node has two children, which is not true in general binary trees.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

node = TreeNode(5)
value = node.val          # Access value
if node.left:             # Check left child
    left_child = node.left  # Retrieve left child
if node.right:            # Check right child
    right_child = node.right  # Retrieve right child

examples.input: Node: val=10, left=Node(5), right=Node(15)
examples.output: Value: 10, left child value: 5, right child value: 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Access node.val → 10.
Step 2: Check node.left → not null, so retrieve node.left.val → 5.
Step 3: Check node.right → not null, so retrieve node.right.val → 15.
Output: 10, 5, 15.

examples.input: Node: val=7, left=null, right=Node(9)
examples.output: Value: 7, left child: null, right child value: 9
examples.trace:
Step 1: Access node.val → 7.
Step 2: Check node.left → null, so no left child.
Step 3: Check node.right → not null, so retrieve node.right.val → 9.
Output: 7, null, 9.

testCases.expected: Value: alpha, left: beta, right: gamma
Value: XYZ, left: null, right: ABC

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure and Node Representation
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree is and how to represent a single node.
ladders.explanation:
A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. It starts with a root node and branches out, forming a tree-like shape. This structure is fundamental in computer science for organizing data efficiently. Each node typically contains a value (or data) and pointers/references to its children. In many programming languages, a node is represented as an object or struct with fields for the value and child references.

Key concepts:
- Node: The basic unit with a value and up to two child references.
- Root: The topmost node from which the tree originates.
- Leaf: A node with no children.
- Binary tree property: Each node has 0, 1, or 2 children.

Intuition: Binary trees enable efficient data storage and retrieval, and traversals like preorder explore all nodes systematically. Understanding this structure is prerequisite for any tree algorithm.

Representation example: In Python, a simple class can define a tree node.

Time complexity: Not applicable for this conceptual level.
Space complexity: O(1) per node.

Connection to L4: This foundation leads to accessing node values and children; L4 builds on this by using the node structure.

Common mistakes:
- Confusing binary trees with other structures like linked lists or arrays.
- Not initializing child references as null/None.
- Misunderstanding the hierarchical nature.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val        # Store the value
        self.left = None      # Reference to left child, initially null
        self.right = None     # Reference to right child, initially null

# Example: Create a simple tree
root = TreeNode(1)           # Root node with value 1
root.left = TreeNode(2)      # Left child with value 2
root.right = TreeNode(3)     # Right child with value 3

examples.input: Create a tree with root value 10, left child 5, right child 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with val=10.
Step 2: Create left child node with val=5, assign to root.left.
Step 3: Create right child node with val=15, assign to root.right.
Result: A binary tree with three nodes.

examples.input: Create a tree with root value A, no children
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with val=A.
Step 2: Leave root.left as None (null).
Step 3: Leave root.right as None (null).
Result: A single-node tree (leaf node).

testCases.input: Root value: 8, left child value: 3, right child value: 10
Root value: X, left child value: Y, right child value: Z
Root value: true, right child value: false
Root value: apple, left child value: banana, right child value: cherry
Root value: 0, left child value: -5, right child value: 5
Root value: 99, left child value: 33, right child value: 66

approaches.name: Approach 3: Morris Traversal O(1) Space
approaches.summary: Modify the tree structure temporarily using threaded binary tree concepts to traverse without stack or recursion. Restore the original structure after traversal. Achieves O(1) extra space by reusing null pointers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Morris Preorder Traversal with O(1) Space
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of preorder traversal using Morris algorithm without stack or recursion.
ladders.explanation:
The Morris traversal algorithm achieves O(1) extra space by temporarily modifying the tree structure using threaded binary tree concepts. The key insight is to reuse null right pointers to create temporary links to successors, allowing us to traverse the tree without additional data structures. For preorder traversal, we need to print nodes in root-left-right order while maintaining this threading mechanism.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize current = root
2. While current is not null:
   a. If current has no left child:
      - Print current
      - Move to right child (current = current.right)
   b. Else (current has left child):
      - Find the inorder predecessor of current (rightmost node in left subtree)
      - If predecessor.right is null:
         * Print current (preorder position)
         * Set predecessor.right = current (create thread)
         * Move to left child (current = current.left)
      - Else (predecessor.right == current):
         * Set predecessor.right = null (remove thread)
         * Move to right child (current = current.right)

Time Complexity: O(n) - Each node is visited at most twice (once to create thread, once to remove it)
Space Complexity: O(1) - Only uses a few pointers, no stack or recursion

Connection to parent: This is the complete solution that combines all the building blocks.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to restore threads - leads to infinite loops
2. Printing nodes at wrong positions in the algorithm
3. Not handling edge cases like empty tree or single node

Real code pattern:
def morris_preorder(root):
    current = root
    while current:
        if not current.left:
            print(current.val)
            current = current.right
        else:
            predecessor = current.left
            while predecessor.right and predecessor.right != current:
                predecessor = predecessor.right
            
            if not predecessor.right:
                print(current.val)  # Preorder print
                predecessor.right = current
                current = current.left
            else:
                predecessor.right = None
                current = current.right

examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, has left child 2
        Find predecessor: from 2, go right to 5, predecessor=5
        predecessor.right is null, print 1, set 5.right=1, current=2
Step 2: current=2, has left child 4
        Find predecessor: from 4, go right (none), predecessor=4
        predecessor.right is null, print 2, set 4.right=2, current=4
Step 3: current=4, no left child, print 4, current=4.right=2
Step 4: current=2, has left child 4
        Find predecessor: from 4, go right to 2, predecessor=4
        predecessor.right=2 (equals current), set 4.right=null, current=2.right=5
Step 5: current=5, no left child, print 5, current=5.right=1
Step 6: current=1, has left child 2
        Find predecessor: from 2, go right to 5, predecessor=5
        predecessor.right=1 (equals current), set 5.right=null, current=1.right=3
Step 7: current=3, has left child (none), print 3, current=3.right=6
Step 8: current=6, no left child, print 6, current=6.right=null
Done. Output: 1 2 4 5 3 6

examples.input:
Tree: [1,null,2,3] (level order)
   1
    \
     2
    /
   3
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, no left child, print 1, current=1.right=2
Step 2: current=2, has left child 3
        Find predecessor: from 3, go right (none), predecessor=3
        predecessor.right is null, print 2, set 3.right=2, current=3
Step 3: current=3, no left child, print 3, current=3.right=2
Step 4: current=2, has left child 3
        Find predecessor: from 3, go right to 2, predecessor=3
        predecessor.right=2 (equals current), set 3.right=null, current=2.right=null
Done. Output: 1 2 3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Inorder Predecessor with Thread Detection
ladders.desc: Locate the rightmost node in left subtree and detect existing threads.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most critical helper function in Morris traversal. The inorder predecessor of a node is the rightmost node in its left subtree. In Morris traversal, we need to find this predecessor to either:
1. Create a thread from it to the current node (when no thread exists)
2. Detect that a thread already exists (when we've visited this path before)

Why this works: In a binary tree, the inorder predecessor of a node is the last node visited before that node in an inorder traversal. By finding this node and temporarily linking it back to the current node, we create a "thread" that allows us to return to the current node after processing the left subtree.

Algorithm:
Given current node:
1. Start with predecessor = current.left
2. While predecessor.right exists AND predecessor.right ≠ current:
   - Move predecessor = predecessor.right
3. Return predecessor

Time Complexity: O(h) in worst case where h is height, but amortized O(1) per node
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to level above: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main Morris loop to manage thread creation and detection.

Common mistakes:
1. Infinite loop if not checking predecessor.right ≠ current
2. Assuming predecessor always exists (need to handle null left child case separately)
3. Not updating predecessor correctly when moving right

Real code pattern:
def find_predecessor(current):
    if not current.left:
        return None
    
    predecessor = current.left
    while predecessor.right and predecessor.right != current:
        predecessor = predecessor.right
    return predecessor

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,6]
Current node: 1
examples.trace:
Step 1: predecessor = 1.left = 2
Step 2: predecessor.right exists? 2.right = 5, not null
        predecessor.right != current? 5 != 1, true
        Move predecessor = 2.right = 5
Step 3: predecessor.right exists? 5.right = null, false
        Loop ends
Result: predecessor = 5

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,6]
Current node: 2 (after thread created from 5 to 1)
examples.trace:
Step 1: predecessor = 2.left = 4
Step 2: predecessor.right exists? 4.right = 2 (thread), not null
        predecessor.right != current? 2 == 2, false
        Loop ends immediately
Result: predecessor = 4 (and we detect thread exists since 4.right == current)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Thread Creation and Restoration Cycle
ladders.desc: The mechanism of temporarily modifying tree structure and restoring it.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central insight of Morris traversal: we can traverse a binary tree without stack or recursion by temporarily modifying the tree structure using threads, then restoring it. A thread is a temporary link from the inorder predecessor of a node back to the node itself.

Why this works: In a binary tree, every node (except leaves) has null right pointers in its left subtree's rightmost nodes. We can reuse these null pointers to store temporary links back to ancestor nodes. This creates a cycle: we go down the left subtree, then use the thread to return to the ancestor, then go to the right subtree.

The cycle has two phases:
1. Thread Creation Phase (first visit):
   - When we first encounter a node with a left child
   - We find its inorder predecessor
   - We create a thread from predecessor.right to current node
   - This allows us to return to current after processing left subtree

2. Thread Restoration Phase (second visit):
   - When we encounter the same node again via the thread
   - We remove the thread (set predecessor.right = null)
   - We process the right subtree

This cycle ensures each node is visited exactly twice (once to create thread, once to remove it), giving us O(n) time complexity while using only O(1) extra space.

Connection to level above: This logic is implemented using the predecessor finding subroutine and controls the main traversal flow.

Common mistakes:
1. Creating threads incorrectly (wrong predecessor)
2. Not restoring threads (memory leaks/infinite loops)
3. Processing nodes in wrong order relative to thread operations

Real code pattern:
# Thread creation phase
if predecessor.right is None:
    # Create thread
    predecessor.right = current
    current = current.left

# Thread restoration phase  
else:  # predecessor.right == current
    # Remove thread
    predecessor.right = None
    current = current.right

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3]
Current node: 1 (first visit)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Find predecessor of 1 = 2
Step 2: Check 2.right = null
Step 3: Create thread: 2.right = 1
Step 4: Move to left child: current = 2
State: Tree temporarily has cycle: 2 → 1

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3] with thread 2.right=1
Current node: 1 (second visit via thread)
examples.output: Remove thread, move to right child
examples.trace:
Step 1: Find predecessor of 1 = 2
Step 2: Check 2.right = 1 (equals current)
Step 3: Remove thread: 2.right = null
Step 4: Move to right child: current = 3
State: Tree restored to original: 2.right = null

ladders.title: Building Block — Inorder Predecessor in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Understanding and finding the node that comes immediately before a given node in inorder traversal.
ladders.explanation:
The inorder predecessor of a node in a binary tree is the node that would be visited immediately before that node in an inorder traversal (left-root-right). For a node with a left child, its inorder predecessor is the rightmost node in its left subtree. If a node has no left child, its inorder predecessor is its first ancestor for which the node is in the right subtree.

Why this matters for Morris traversal: We use the inorder predecessor to create temporary threads. When we're at a node, we find its inorder predecessor in the left subtree and temporarily link it back to the current node. This creates a path to return to the current node after we finish processing the left subtree.

Finding the predecessor (when node has left child):
1. Start at the left child
2. Keep moving right until you can't go right anymore
3. That rightmost node is the inorder predecessor

Visual intuition: Imagine doing an inorder traversal. When you visit a node, the last node you visited from its left subtree is its inorder predecessor.

Time Complexity: O(h) where h is height of left subtree
Space Complexity: O(1) if done iteratively

Connection to level above: This concept is essential for the thread creation mechanism in Morris traversal.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing inorder predecessor with other types of predecessors
2. Not handling the case where node has no left child
3. Assuming the predecessor is always the left child (it's the rightmost in left subtree)

Real code pattern:
def inorder_predecessor(node):
    if not node or not node.left:
        return None
    
    current = node.left
    while current.right:
        current = current.right
    return current

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,6]
Find inorder predecessor of node 1
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 1 has left child 2
Step 2: Start at left child: current = 2
Step 3: current.right exists? 2.right = 5, move current = 5
Step 4: current.right exists? 5.right = null, stop
Result: inorder predecessor of 1 is 5

examples.input:
Tree: [10,5,15,3,7,12,18]
Find inorder predecessor of node 5
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 5 has left child 3
Step 2: Start at left child: current = 3
Step 3: current.right exists? 3.right = null, stop
Result: inorder predecessor of 5 is 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Traversal with Pointer Manipulation
ladders.desc: Navigating a binary tree using pointers and modifying pointer connections.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves moving through a binary tree structure using node pointers and temporarily modifying the connections between nodes. In a binary tree, each node typically has pointers to its left and right children (and possibly a parent). The basic operations are:

1. Moving down the tree: current = current.left or current = current.right
2. Checking for null pointers: if current.left is None
3. Modifying pointers: node.right = other_node

Why this is fundamental: Morris traversal relies heavily on pointer manipulation. We need to:
- Navigate the tree without recursion or stack
- Temporarily change right pointers to create threads
- Restore pointers to their original values

Pointer manipulation in trees requires careful attention to avoid losing references or creating cycles that can't be undone. The key is to always save necessary information before modifying pointers.

Time Complexity: O(1) for each pointer operation
Space Complexity: O(1) for the pointers themselves

Connection to level above: Pointer manipulation is how we implement the thread creation and restoration cycle.

Common mistakes:
1. Losing the original pointer value when modifying
2. Creating cycles that can't be traversed
3. Not checking for null pointers before dereferencing
4. Modifying pointers in wrong order

Real code pattern:
# Basic pointer operations
current = root  # Start at root

# Move left if possible
if current.left:
    current = current.left

# Move right if possible
if current.right:
    current = current.right

# Modify a pointer (create thread)
temp = current.right  # Save original if needed
current.right = target_node

# Restore a pointer
current.right = temp  # or None

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3]
Operation: Move from root to left child, then modify right pointer
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start: current = 1
Step 2: Check 1.left = 2 (not null)
Step 3: Move left: current = 1.left = 2
Step 4: Save original: original_right = 2.right = null
Step 5: Modify pointer: 2.right = 1
State: current at node 2, 2.right points to 1 (thread created)

examples.input:
Tree: [1,2,3] with 2.right=1
Operation: Restore pointer and move right from root
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start: current = 1
Step 2: Check 1.right = 3 (not null)
Step 3: Before moving, restore thread from predecessor:
        Find node with thread to 1 (node 2)
        Set 2.right = null
Step 4: Move right: current = 1.right = 3
State: current at node 3, tree restored

testCases.input: Tree: [10,5,15], Start: 10, Operation: move left then right

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Pointers
ladders.desc: Understanding how binary trees are represented using nodes with left and right pointers.
ladders.explanation:
A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children, typically called left and right. In most programming languages, binary trees are represented using node objects that contain:
1. A value (the data stored in the node)
2. A pointer/reference to the left child node
3. A pointer/reference to the right child node

Why this is fundamental: All tree traversal algorithms, including Morris traversal, rely on this basic structure. The pointers (left and right) define the connections between nodes, creating the tree structure. A null pointer indicates no child in that direction.

Basic concepts:
- Root: The topmost node (no parent)
- Leaf: A node with no children (both pointers are null)
- Internal node: A node with at least one child
- Parent-child relationship: If node A has a pointer to node B, then A is parent of B
- Path: A sequence of nodes connected by parent-child relationships

Visualizing pointers: Think of each node as a box with two arrows pointing to other boxes (or to nothing/null). Traversal means following these arrows in a specific order.

Time Complexity: Accessing a node's children is O(1)
Space Complexity: Each node stores 2 pointers + data

Connection to level above: Understanding this structure is prerequisite for any pointer manipulation in trees.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Confusing node value with node reference
2. Not understanding that null means "no child"
3. Thinking trees are stored contiguously in memory (they're linked structures)
4. Not visualizing the pointer connections

Real code pattern (Python-style):
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0):
        self.val = val          # Node value
        self.left = None       # Pointer to left child
        self.right = None      # Pointer to right child

# Creating a simple tree:
#     1
#    / \
#   2   3
root = TreeNode(1)
root.left = TreeNode(2)
root.right = TreeNode(3)

# Accessing:
left_child = root.left    # Gets node with value 2
right_child = root.right  # Gets node with value 3

examples.input: Create tree: root=1, left child=2, right child=3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with value 1
        root = TreeNode(1)
Step 2: Create left child node with value 2
        left_node = TreeNode(2)
Step 3: Connect root to left child
        root.left = left_node
Step 4: Create right child node with value 3
        right_node = TreeNode(3)
Step 5: Connect root to right child
        root.right = right_node
Result: Tree: 1 → (left: 2, right: 3)
        2 → (left: null, right: null)
        3 → (left: null, right: null)

examples.input:
Given tree: root=10 with left=5 and right=15
Access left child's value
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root node (value 10)
Step 2: Follow left pointer: root.left
Step 3: This gives us the left child node
Step 4: Access the value: root.left.val
Result: 5

testCases.input: Create single node tree with value 5
Tree: Node(1) with left=Node(2), get left child value
Tree: Node(1) with right=Node(3), get right child value
Tree: Node(10) with left=Node(5), right=Node(15), get root value
Tree: Node(10) with left=Node(5), right=Node(15), check if left child exists
Tree: Node(10) with left=Node(5), right=Node(15), check if left.left exists
Tree: Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3), count pointers from root
Tree: Node(4) with left=null, right=Node(6), check if leaf
Tree: Node(7) with left=null, right=null, check if leaf
